epigraph.text:
Two are better than one, because they have a good 
return for their labor.

content.text:
Angles don't exist in isolation. Understanding how angles relate to 
each other—as complements, supplements, or pairs formed by 
intersecting lines—is essential for solving geometric problems.

content.title: Complementary Angles
content.content:
Two angles are **complementary** if 
their measures add up to $90°$.

content.title: Finding a complement
content.problem:
Find the complement of each angle:

1. $30°$

2. $55°$

3. $72°$

content.title: Supplementary Angles
content.content:
Two angles are **supplementary** if 
their measures add up to $180°$.

content.title: Finding a supplement
content.problem:
Find the supplement of each angle:

1. $60°$

2. $115°$

3. $90°$
content.solution:
1. Supplement $= 180° - 60° = 120°$

2. Supplement $= 180° - 115° = 65°$

3. Supplement $= 180° - 90° = 90°$ (a right angle is its own supplement!)

content.content:
Complementary angles complete each other to form a right angle. 
Similarly, in the body of Christ, "The eye cannot say to the hand, 
`I don't need you!' " (1 Corinthians 12:21). Different gifts 
complement each other to form a complete whole.

content.text: When two lines intersect, they form two pairs of **vertical angles**.

content.title: Vertical Angles
content.content:
**Vertical angles** are the non-adjacent 
angles formed when two lines intersect. Vertical angles are always 
equal in measure.

content.title: Vertical angles
content.problem:
Two lines intersect, forming four angles. If one angle measures $70°$, 
find the measures of the other three angles.
content.solution:
Let the four angles be $\angle 1$, $\angle 2$, $\angle 3$, $\angle 4$ 
going clockwise. If $\angle 1 = 70°$:

- $\angle 3 = 70°$ (vertical to $\angle 1$)

- $\angle 2 = 180° - 70° = 110°$ (supplementary to $\angle 1$)

- $\angle 4 = 110°$ (vertical to $\angle 2$)

content.text: We can use algebra to find unknown angle measures.

content.title: Complementary angle equation
content.problem:
Two complementary angles have measures $x$ and $(2x + 15)°$. Find both 
angles.
content.solution:
Since complementary angles sum to $90°$:
$$
x + (2x + 15) &= 90 \\
3x + 15 &= 90 \\
3x &= 75 \\
x &= 25
$$

First angle: $25°$. Second angle: $2(25) + 15 = 65°$.

Check: $25° + 65° = 90°$

content.title: Supplementary angle equation
content.problem:
Two supplementary angles have measures $(3x - 10)°$ and $(x + 30)°$. 
Find both angles.

content.title: Scripture
content.content:
The precision required in angle measurement echoes God's instructions 
for the tabernacle: "Make this tabernacle and all its furnishings 
exactly like the pattern I will show you" (Exodus 25:9). Exact 
measurements matter in geometry as in sacred architecture.

marginNotes.text:
Just as a body, though one, has 
many parts, but all its many parts form one body.

As iron sharpens iron, so one person 
sharpens another.

I will make justice the measuring line 
and righteousness the plumb line.

marginNotes.author: Archimedes
marginNotes.text:
Give me a place to stand, and I will move 
the earth.

marginNotes.author: David Hilbert
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is a game played according to 
certain simple rules with meaningless marks on paper.
